## Details

| Primary Bucket ID | [`f9a4e690-4f46-5720-8ddc-ad6e4362233e`](https://errors.code.visualstudio.com/bucket/f9a4e690-4f46-5720-8ddc-ad6e4362233e?product=VSCode) |
| Related Bucket ID | [`ea8af029-2149-4b37-513c-8d86d2220d1b`](https://errors.code.visualstudio.com/bucket/ea8af029-2149-4b37-513c-8d86d2220d1b?product=VSCode) |

| Last Seen | 2026-03-23 |
| Combined Hits | 2.2M |
| Combined Affected Users | 142.5K |
| Platforms | Linux, Mac, Windows |
| Product | VSCode |

## Analysis

Multiple listener LEAK errors originate from `chatTerminalToolProgressPart.ts` in the chat terminal tool progress component. Two distinct leak paths are observed:

1. **Bucket `f9a4e690`** (1.2M hits / 64.9K users): Leak occurs during construction at line 1082 and line 356, triggered via `createToolInvocationSubPart` in `chatToolInvocationPart.ts`.
2. **Bucket `ea8af029`** (1M hits / 77.6K users): Leak occurs in `_initializeTerminalActions` at line 596, triggered via the `onDidContinueInBackground` event listener.

title: Cmd+key shortcuts stop working in terminal editor after running a TUI application
body:
<!-- ⚠️⚠️ Do Not Delete This! bug_report_template ⚠️⚠️ -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- 🕮 Read our guide about submitting issues: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/wiki/Submitting-Bugs-and-Suggestions -->
<!-- 🔎 Search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->
<!-- 🧪 Test using the latest Insiders build to see if your issue has already been fixed: https://code.visualstudio.com/insiders/ -->
<!-- 💡 Instead of creating your report here, use 'Report Issue' from the 'Help' menu in VS Code to pre-fill useful information. -->
<!-- 🔧 Launch with `code --disable-extensions` to check. -->
Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: Yes

<!-- 🪓 If you answered No above, use 'Help: Start Extension Bisect' from Command Palette to try to identify the cause. -->
<!-- 📣 Issues caused by an extension need to be reported directly to the extension publisher. The 'Help > Report Issue' dialog can assist with this. -->
- VS Code Version: 1.112.0
- OS Version: 26.3.1

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Open VS Code's integrated terminal
2. Run any TUI (text user interface) CLI application that uses a modern terminal UI framework (e.g. Claude Code CLI 2.1.83, OpenAI Codex CLI)
3. Press Cmd+Q, Cmd+T, Cmd+Y, or other Cmd+key shortcuts
4. Observe: the bare letter ("q", "t", "y") is typed into the TUI's input instead of VS Code executing the shortcut
5. Cmd+P and Cmd+Shift+P still work (these are on VS Code's always-intercept list)
6. Exit the TUI application (Ctrl+C or /exit). The terminal remains broken - Cmd+T now outputs raw escape sequences like `6;9u` instead of executing the shortcut. The terminal does not recover until the tab is closed. 
7. Run a TUI app using an older terminal framework version (e.g. Claude Code CLI 2.1.81) - shortcuts work correctly

Additional context:

Using "Developer: Toggle Keyboard Shortcuts Troubleshooting", the logs show that VS Code's keybinding service resolves the shortcut correctly via "Soft dispatching" but the event "cannot be dispatched":

[KeybindingService]: / Soft dispatching keyboard event
[KeybindingService]: | Resolving meta+[KeyC]
[KeybindingService]: \ From 11 keybinding entries, matched editor.action.clipboardCopyAction, when: no when condition, source: built-in.

Despite the match, the command is not intercepted and the raw keystroke leaks to the terminal, where the Cmd modifier is stripped and only the bare letter reaches the TUI application.

This affects multiple unrelated TUI CLIs (Claude Code, OpenAI Codex), suggesting the issue is in how VS Code's terminal handles keyboard dispatch for applications that set certain terminal modes.
Older versions of the same CLIs (using older TUI framework versions) are not affected.

Relevant settings:
 - terminal.integrated.sendKeybindingsToShell: false
 - terminal.integrated.allowChords: true

title: Copilot CLI : MCP tools not honored in Custom Agents
body:
When the Copilot CLI runs as a third-party agent inside VS Code, custom agents invoked as sub-agents do not receive the tools declared in their `.agent.md` definition files. The agents are restricted to a limited read-only set (file viewing only), regardless of what the `tools` property specifies.

The same agents work correctly when the CLI runs natively in the terminal, where they receive full access to their declared tools including GitHub MCP tools and terminal execution.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Define a custom agent in `~/.copilot/agents/` with specific tools declared in the YAML frontmatter (e.g., `github/search_issues`, `read`).
2. Start the Copilot CLI as a third-party agent inside VS Code.
3. Ask the CLI to delegate a task to the custom agent.
4. Observe that the sub-agent reports having only file-viewing tools (grep, glob, view) and cannot access its declared tools.
5. Run the same agent from the CLI natively in the terminal. The agent receives all declared tools and works as expected.

## Additional testing

- Removing the `tools` property entirely (which per the [custom agents configuration docs](https://docs.github.com/en/copilot/reference/custom-agents-configuration#tools) enables all available tools) produces the same restricted behavior.
- Setting Copilot Chat to "Bypass Approvals" permission level does not change the outcome. The sub-agent still only receives read-only file tools.

These results suggest the restriction is not related to tool naming, agent configuration, or permission settings, but to how VS Code mediates tool access for third-party agents.

## Expected behavior

Custom agents invoked as sub-agents should receive the tools declared in their agent definition, regardless of whether the CLI is running natively or inside VS Code.

## Actual behavior

The VS Code host restricts sub-agents to a limited read-only tool set, silently ignoring the `tools` property from the agent definition. No error or warning is surfaced.

## Environment

- VS Code Insiders 1.113.0-insider
- OS: Linux x64 (WSL2)

title: Copilot Chat: File editing tools and MCP tool calls produce mojibake when strings contain Unicode characters
body:
Description:

When Copilot Chat passes strings containing non-ASCII Unicode characters through its tool infrastructure, the characters arrive double-encoded (mojibake). This affects:

  1. replace_string_in_file / multi_replace_string_in_file -- the newString parameter is corrupted when written to disk
  2. MCP tool call parameters -- any str parameter passed to an MCP server arrives corrupted (e.g., posting a comment via an ADO MCP tool)

The corruption pattern is consistent with UTF-8 bytes being decoded as Latin-1/Windows-1252, then re-encoded as UTF-8.

Repro steps (file editing):

  1. Open a markdown file that already contains Unicode characters (e.g., ❌, ✅, --, ⚠️, →)
  2. Ask Copilot to edit the file, adding new text that includes Unicode characters
  3. Observe the written file

Repro steps (MCP tool):

  1. Create an MCP server with a tool that accepts a comment: str parameter and logs the received value
  2. Ask a Copilot agent (chat mode) to call the tool with text containing Unicode characters
  3. Observe the received string at the MCP server

Expected: Unicode characters are preserved correctly.

Actual: Characters are double-encoded / mojibake:

Intended Written/Received Unicode
— (em dash) â€" U+2014
❌ â\u009dŒ U+274C
✅ âœ… U+2705
⚠️ âš ï¸\u0090 U+26A0
→ â†' U+2192
’ (right quote) â€™ U+2019
🔴 ðŸ"´ U+1F534

Note: Some corrupted emoji contain lone surrogates (e.g., \uDC9D), which are invalid in UTF-8/JSON and can cause downstream API calls to fail entirely (not just display incorrectly).

Impact:

  • File editing: Cannot reliably write Unicode into files via editing tools. Written content arrives with mojibake.
  • MCP tools: Any MCP tool receiving Unicode strings gets corrupted input. In our case, ADO REST API rejects comment posts containing invalid UTF-8 (lone surrogates), or posts unreadable comments.
  • Workaround required: Either use ASCII-only substitutes (e.g., -- instead of --, [X] instead of ❌, -> instead of →), or use terminal commands (python -c "..." scripts) to edit files, completely bypassing the editing tools.

title: "Add Element to Chat" screenshot rectangle is misaligned
body: Repro steps: - Open Integrated Browser and browse to a page - Change the zoom level of the page itself (not of VS Code) so that it's a different zoom level than VS Code itself - Use Add Element to Chat to add an element to chat - 🐛 The attached screenshot will not be aligned with the element you clicked on I'm using: - VS Code Version: 1.113.0-insider (Universal) - OS Version: Darwin arm64 25.3.0 --- See issue here: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/303645#issuecomment-4111827396 ### Current behavior (notice the screenshot rectangle is not aligned with the image element): ### Expected (screenshot rectangle aligned with the image element): --- **Probable cause:** It seems like Add Element to Chat uses the VS Code zoom level when calculating the rectangle for the screenshot, instead of the browser's own zoom level. **Probable solution:** In integrated browser (anything related with /browserView/), use the browser's own zoom level for calculating the rectangle. For simple browser or Live Preview extension, use VS Code's zoom level (since those don't support a browser-specific zoom level).

title: `auxiliaryBarFocus` is always `false`
body:
<!-- ⚠️⚠️ Do Not Delete This! bug_report_template ⚠️⚠️ -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- 🕮 Read our guide about submitting issues: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/wiki/Submitting-Bugs-and-Suggestions -->
<!-- 🔎 Search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->
<!-- 🧪 Test using the latest Insiders build to see if your issue has already been fixed: https://code.visualstudio.com/insiders/ -->
<!-- 💡 Instead of creating your report here, use 'Report Issue' from the 'Help' menu in VS Code to pre-fill useful information. -->

In the current [VSCode documentation](https://code.visualstudio.com/api/references/when-clause-contexts#visible-view-container-when-clause-context) there is indication for the flag `auxiliaryBarFocus` which should be true when the auxiliary bar is on focus, false otherwise.

This flag is actually always `false`.

For instance, i tried to map a unique key combination:

```json
 {
 "key": "cmd+shift+9",
 "command": "workbench.action.previousAuxiliaryBarView",
 "when": "auxiliaryBarFocus",
 },
```

to check that it actually works, but when i'm on focus and i hit this shortcut, nothing happens.

To make sure that the shortcut would actually do something, I changed the when condition to `"when": "!editorFocus && !terminalFocus && !sideBarFocus"` and then it worked.

I've found the culprit by running an inspection in VSCode and, after clicking within the auxiliary bar, the flag is always set to `false`.

<!-- 🔧 Launch with `code --disable-extensions` to check. -->
Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: Yes

<!-- 🪓 If you answered No above, use 'Help: Start Extension Bisect' from Command Palette to try to identify the cause. -->
<!-- 📣 Issues caused by an extension need to be reported directly to the extension publisher. The 'Help > Report Issue' dialog can assist with this. -->
- VS Code Version: `1.111.0`
- OS Version: macOS 26.3.1

Steps to Reproduce:

1. set any action with `auxiliaryBarFocus` as when condition;
2. find out is never activated.

title: Sessions App: Deleting a Fork conversation deletes work tree of original conversation
body:
In the Sessions App:

1. start a local session with several turns and file modifications
2. go to a a previous user message in the conversation and choose "Fork conversation from this point"
3. delete forked conversation
4. answer yes when asked to delete the work tree

-> Original conversation is busted since the fork and the original conversation shared a work tree.

I would have expected one of those things:
a) have an option to create a new work tree when forking
b) we warned that the work tree is shared between the original and the forked conversation
c) not being asked to delete the work tree at all because it is shared

title: Sandboxing should not attempt running if bubblewrap and socat are missing
body: * Create a fresh WSL installation * Open VS Code and connect to it * Enable sandboxing * Use `Please run ls and show me file sizes too` * Observe how it fails We know up-front that the environment we're connected to doesn't have these dependencies. We should avoid attempting to execute a sandboxed command only to discover the dependencies are missing. Instead, we should proactively surface this missing dependency in a pleasant way to the user and also inform the LLM that sandboxing doesn't work due to this missing deps, maybe the LLM can help the user set them up.

title: Terminal tool output shows up in multiple places and is confusing
body:
I see this very often, the same terminal output scrolling in 2 or 3 different places and it's confusing what's going on with all the movement and expanding and collapsing. Not sure that's by design or a bug.
